sentences.text: My name is Leo Pop, P-O-P.
That's right.
October 4, 1934.
That's 61.
I was born in Amsterdam in the Netherlands.
I have lived till 1943 in Amsterdam.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We all lived-- I mean all, I had a brother.
And we lived in Amsterdam in the city in a stor-- three-story home.
We lived on the third floor.
And most of the relatives, my grandparents from both sides, uncles and aunts, lived all in a [INAUDIBLE] a direction close to where we are, around maybe 5 miles at the most.
My father was a sales representative for cosmetic products.
And he worked, basically, out of Amsterdam.
His name was Solomon.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So.
And my mother's name was Ann, Anna.
And my brother's name was Johnny.
My brother was four years younger.
He was born in 1938.
So I was four years by myself before he was.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The-- uh-- well, my parents were really, was very close family with the relatives, my grandparents.
We spent quite a bit of time visiting each other, and my uncles and aunts.
My father had three brothers who all were married.
My mother had, um, two brothers and three sisters.
And, like I had mentioned before, we all lived relatively close.
One of my sister's mother had a daughter, was my niece Rita, who also survived the war after.
And that's basically the only one of the whole family besides my aunt Dorothy, that was the younger sister of my mother.
Not really religious, no.
Uh, we celebrated, or, you know, whatever you want to call it, the, the Jewish Passover.
And we did Hanukkah, and so on.
You know, we celebrated those, but not that we really went religious and went to synagogue.
I remember having gone there once or twice with-- not even with my parents, but with the neighbors downstairs who lived on the second floor.
Before the war?
No, before the war, no.
There was nothing.
Because it was, yeah, quite a large district in Amsterdam, the Jewish district, really.
What, by the way, is all broken down now and completely disappeared.
But it was quite active.
And, you know, we all-- we went quite often down there to visit because one of my father's brothers lived near there, too.
He, he was a principal of one of the schools and, you know, we went to visit him quite often.

Well, as far as I remember, we-- I went to kindergarten, uh, what was-- as it happened to be the location of that kindergarten was just behind the museum, or museum, I would say the-- the building where later on the Jewish people were all gathered before they were put on transports.
But just as it happened to be, but of course I went to that kindergarten before the war.
And that was in 19-- when did I go to kindergarten, you know, up to '38, '39 I guess.
Then in 1940 I went to, when I turned six I went to the normal school to start grade one.
And as it turned out I went just there for, I guess, one and a half years.
And that time the Germans stopped, you know, Jewish people going to school, and that's how it was.
I never attended any more than the, you know, first and part of the second grade.
Well--
Well, Nazism, that of course came up.
And I know my-- not really.
I was too young, really, to realize what that was.
But I know my parents, especially my father, talking with his brothers and, you know, with his brother-in-laws about, you know, the German.
But that was about it.
I mean, you know they hoped it would be the same as in 1914, that Holland would be spared.
Well, it was in 1940.
And I remember being with my parents on the balcony of the house, on the third level.
And we could see the planes come over.
Living in Amsterdam we were, fortunately, not bombed.
But we saw the German planes come and go.
And it didn't take long after that, a couple days, before they really entered the city.
And, you know, that was--
Well, not really, no.
Not in particular.
Because, you know, like I say, I was six years old in 1940, or five years.
No, I don't remember the words, although, you know, gradually grew on you what-- what it meant.
Well, it didn't take long before they really started to, um, start putting down laws.
Like, you know, we, we were not allowed to have radios.
You had to turn them all in.
You had to-- no bikes, all this.
And, you know, then gradually they started to limit your-- you know, where you went and what you did.

And then-- and then, of course, in 19-- I guess it was '42 or '43 that they came out that you had-- Jewish peoples over six years old had to wear the Star of David that more or less, you know, showed to everybody, you know, where you-- where you were and who you were.
Uh, yeah, I remember kind of vaguely wearing it for the first time.
Actually at that time, being young, you kind of thought it would be-- you know, it was kind of an honor, you know, to be as such.
You know, that you had to wear a Star of David because you were Jewish.
And I remember one of my brothers of my mother got married in front of a synagogue, where I have a picture of later on, and there I had to wear the Star of David.
I had to wear the Star of David to play outside, and all this.
And that had bad consequences because you had so many kids who really, you know, had it in for you and, you know, gave you a rough time.
Well, all kind of-- you know, dirty Jew and all this.
And, you know, I remember having a ball and, you know, they took it away and there was nothing you could do because, you know, you were not supposed to play.
We were not-- I was not supposed to be, as Jewish, to go in the parks anymore because that was all-- you know, not allowed.
And we lived close to a-- to a park, and I couldn't even go in and play in the sandbox, or whatever it was, you know.
We were not allowed to go in the park.
So you had to stay kind of on the street where everybody could see you.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
So in that-- well, that was basically in 19-- not right away in '40, but '41, '42 it started to become that way.
And then in the end of '42, they started the razias, what was basically the pickups for the-- where they started to take care of the Jewish people and pick them up from the street, originally.
When you had the star they could easily see that.
So and then later on they came and picked up the people what they had the list of and went to the houses.
So tell us what happened to your family at that time.
Well, um, I remember some of the relatives being picked up.
Where I lived in Amsterdam, we had a square.
And that's where they had kind of-- more or less, they gathered them.
They had the trucks there.
And, uh, I have seen a couple of my uncles and aunts being put on transports right there.
And there was nothing you could do, of course, well, you know, German have weapons, and all this.
So, you know, I know-- I know a couple uncles and aunts who were picked up, picked out of the houses, with my nieces and that.
And they were from there transported to the downtown in Amsterdam from where they put on transport to the concentration camps.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Gradually, you know, the family when that started-- and there's another thing, we-- where we lived we had an attic where we had, we thought, we could hide-- some hiding places for.
So my uncles and aunts and all this, they started to bring their silverware, and all the jewelry, and all this what they have.
They brought them over to be put up in the attic there in the places.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I know my grandparents brought stuff, my uncles, my aunts.
And, you know, we had quite a bit of-- put down.
It was all behind boards and walls.
And then coming back after the war to that house there was nothing left, already.
Somewhere along the line the neighbors must have-- they knew about it, too.
So they must have taken care of it.
But that's beside the point.
So then gradually, I could-- you know, my family became smaller and smaller.
My grandparents from my father's side were picked up, put up there, downtown, and then put into transportation.
My father found out, and he went down to the, uh, building where they are all housed.
And he was trying to get them out, to buy them out one way or another.
He was picked up and put on transport.
And he escaped.
He came home one night, all damaged and band-- wounded because he had jumped out of the train going down to, I guess, Westerbork or whatever concentration camp there was in Holland.
And then they went from there to Czechoslovakia, or whatever.
The idea was, what I was told that-- no, not often, but-- they wanted to exchange the Jewish people from Poland with the ones in Holland.
And that's what-- why they put those, picked up the people, just to bring them-- that's what we were told by the Germans and by the news media there in Holland.
They just wanted to exchange them, bring the Polish Jewish over to Holland, and we had to go to Poland.
Well, maybe in the beginning.
Because at that time they asked for volunteers.
I know none of my family ever-- you know, relatives, went as volunteers.
Not till they were picked up later on.
Well, for my mo-- uh, my mother's side it was Jonas and Betty, Betje.
My father's side was Leopold, what I am called after, and my grandmother was Judy.
My parent's grandp-- uh, my father's grandp-- my father's parents were picked up, you know, relatively early in the season.
And later on I found out they were already killed in April 1943 in Sobibor.
My mother's grandparents stayed a little longer.
You know, and I don't know what exactly when they, but they were after.
From time to time the Germans had the big razias in the big cities.
And the underground kept my parents kind of aware of when they were coming.
And--
When the razias were, were planned.
You know, they had some collaborators, I guess, if you want to call it that, in the German organization who told the underground, well, they are planning to do this and that and that.
So, you know, we always-- well, not always, but quite often knew about the plans.
And then you kind of tried to hide, or whatever.
Uh, it became so bad in 1943 when they really started that my parents decided that it was time for my brother and myself to-- to find places and to move on.
My brother went, I guess, in May '43, early May, I guess.
And then the underground started to look for a place for me.
Because they just couldn't pick you up and-- you know, without knowing where they could bring you.
And I know my parents were also waiting for an address to go to.
But as it turns out, they never made it.
No, I didn't.
I was just told he went on holidays.
I was still home.
That was in '43.
I was about eight years old, so.
And he was four, and he was picked up by the underground.
And he went on holidays.
That's what I was told.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then, um-- [SNIFFS] excuse me.
Um, at the-- sometime in June, again, like I mentioned before, we got note that there was going to be a big razia in Amsterdam.
Um, again, the underground came, or someone warned my parents.
And this was a real big one.
All over the whole city, nobody could get in or out, or, you know, whatever it was.
We lived, um, like I say, at the house, a three-story house.
However, under the house was a crawl space, maybe 2 feet high.
And we knew about the crawl space, and we had been there before.
And we-- so we moved in the crawl space.
That was right at the-- when you come into the building was a deck, you know, under the carpet where you could move in.
We moved into there, and we lived there for a couple days.
My father, mother, and myself.
Uh, the neighbors on the second floor knew because they brought us food.
They kind of, you know, when there was nothing, they kind of hit the little deck what was, you know, the opening or the cover, and then we knew there was someone there that wanted to-- you know.
Till finally-- I don't say we have been in there maybe one or two days-- they finally moved to the house where we are, were.
And you could see them come because the crawl space had to be vented and aired.
And one of the German boots was right in front of the crawl space, and we was on the other side of the grill.
And he had the standing there, SS or whatever it was, with the gun and all this.
You heard the other one breaking into the house and going upstairs to the third floor where we lived, supposed to have lived.
The neighbors told them that we had been picked up already.
So they didn't really-- fortunately, didn't start searching all over.
Because they-- the [INAUDIBLE] were all-- you know, they didn't keep any sort of [INAUDIBLE].

So but then what happened is after they left, they put out a seal that that house, you know, people had gone and then the collaborators could go in with one of the German high commands and take out whatever they felt was they could use.
So after they had left, maybe hours later, or the next day, I don't remember, we went back up and there were all the seals.
So we had to break to get into our own house.
And that was, just then my parents really got hold of the underground and said, well, it's time to move.
So it was in a few days I was taken by the underground to my hiding place.
Did your parents prepare you for your being taken?
Well, they just told me it was just for a small holiday.
That's what they, you know.
And they were going to go on holidays themselves, too.
You know, they, they didn't know where I was going, or nothing.
Just that, you know, the underground was, you know, going to take care of it.

And I remember, you know, saying goodbye and moving with the, the g-- the person who picked me up at the house down to the street car without the star because I was-- you know, you couldn't have that on.
At the corner of the square was a laundry of-- you know, washing, for washing facilities.
And I was trying to move back to turn around to see one more look at them.
She says, don't look back because there are all kind of collaborators living around there, too.
And so that's how I moved, went with the underground to the railroad station.
And then in the train, of course, we had inspection all the time.
And I really don't know how they managed to get that done, but they managed to get me down to the southern province.
No, I didn't have false papers as such.
But, you know, it's just-- I don't know what they really had to.
Maybe the underground did have some papers, or whatever.
I never had.
Well, nothing then.
I was just going on holidays.
He was taking me to, you know, to the southern part to go on holidays for a couple-- because in Amsterdam there was, you know, limited food supplies and dangers and all this.
So, you know, I was told I was to go, go on holidays.
And then I ended up in Limburg, in the city of Heerlen.
I was brought to a family [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what had offered to hide Jewish people.
And you know, I got there, of course.
You know, you miss your parents and all this.
But the part was, the next day I was outside on the road.
And hold and below, there I see my brother who had gone a couple weeks before me and I didn't know where he was.
He just happened to be with people right across the street.
So that was, you know, that didn't go, of course.
Since my brother had been already at those people for a couple weeks, they decided right away I had to move again.
And I moved and switched with some other boy who also was from Amsterdam who I also knew because he lived across from my niece in Amsterdam.
And I was, uh, brought to other, another residence where I stayed for the rest of the war.
Can we go back
[SNIFFS]
Surely.
Yeah.
Well, nothing.
I mean, at that time we realized there was-- you know, it was too dangerous to be-- you know, and I, at that time I knew, too, what they were after.
It was just not, you know, holidays like, you know.
Because the danger was right outside.
And, you know, the people on the second floor, they brought us some food, you know, whatever was available, you know, to eat while we was hiding in that place.
Just in the corner.
Because you couldn't get out, you couldn't straighten out.
It was just almost not even 2 feet high.
Well, the light wasn't too bad because we still could see.
You know, we had the daylight through the grills in the concrete wall because the vent, the space.
So that wasn't too bad.
So we could see daylight, and we knew it was night, and.
But, like I said, the German was standing on the outside, and we were not even a foot away, but just hidden by the wall and the grill.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Another thing what I should, probably should have mentioned-- we lived on number three on the street.
And I would say a couple homes over was a German, uh, was SS headquarters.
So you could always see them come in and out and all this.
So, you know, we knew that there was quite a dangers close by in all this as well.
Oh, yeah, we saw-- you know, I've seen enough.
Because those people when they were put on the transportation, they didn't-- well, it was all kind of ages, of course, young people, older people.
And if they didn't move fast enough, they just did the, you know, just [INAUDIBLE] hit them and all this.
I've seen some people being shot, being trying to escape from there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But, you know, I guess you were young and you didn't really-- well, of course, you knew it was, you know, what it was and could happen to anybody.
I mean, you know, if we had been picked up, if we hadn't been hidden in that place we would have been picked up that night, too.
I don't know.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There must have been, there was, you know, quite an active group of the underground in Amsterdam.

Because they also, the underground, that section of the underground what took care of me also, uh, got rid of quite-- got hold of quite a few of the smaller babies from the assembly hall where all the people were put down.
And, you know, one of the nurses in there helped to get them escaped right across the street from where they were brought over.

Because later on, after the war, we found-- you know, heard quite a bit of stories of the Jewish people who had-- who were babies, really, who went from there and were brought also to Limburg, the province of Limburg.
As a matter of fact, one of the kids also who was hidden in Limburg what turned out to be later on the mayor of Amsterdam.
Van Thijn.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he's still quite active in, you know, the Jewish people, as well.
Well, um, as I recall, their name was family Gyzen, G-Y-Z-E-N.
No, I was there just too short.
I don't even know if, you know, it was more than a day.
That was a la-- a single lady.
At that time, she was probably-- it was in '43, so she must have been around in her late 20s or middle 20s-- who lived on the second floor in Heerlen with her sister and her husband downstairs.
And they had children, three children of their own.
And I, she took me in.
Her parents had died at the beginning of the war-- old age, I guess.
And she took me in.
And that's where I stayed during the war, after the war, and actually, you know, till I moved to Canada later on then.
So.
Why?
Well, I guess it probably was a real-- this house where I was, those people, especially downstairs.
It was quite a-- underground was moving in and out of that house.
So they were quite involved with, you know, helping Jewish people, and I guess she decided she should do that, too.
And she did.
She opened her house, and, you know, I was quite welcome there, really.
I don't know, not-- maybe the underground might have paid during the war.
I don't know how that really worked at that time.
I am sure that it was, uh, you know, you needed some money for.
I don't know if my parents had money that they paid.
I couldn't tell you.
But after the war, of course, she was compensated more or less, then.
But during the war, no.
But the underground, then again, came with all kind of coupons because she had to buy food and clothing and all this.
And, you know, you needed coupons.
And the underground, of course, did enough break-ins in the, you know, in the German buildings where they got hold of the stamps, and all this, food stamps.
Uh, well, I was told when I was there that if I, anybody asked that I had to say I came from the bombed-out city of Ram-- Rotterdam.
Rotterdam at the beginning of the war was really bombed.
And, you know, a lot of kids had to-- were moved out because, you know, it was not rebuilt at all.
So I had to tell them that, you know, I was from there.
OK.
